profile.name: Sahil Shingate
profile.bio: Developer passionate about creating scalable and creative web solutions. Learning and building with MERN stack, Python, and Data Structures & Algorithms in C++.

prs.title: Fix: Use 18 physicochemical properties instead of 21 in AptaNet (Issue #173)
prs.body:
Hey everyone! 👋

I was going through the open issues and came across #173. 
After reading the original AptaNet paper, I noticed that 
the best results actually come from using 18 properties 
(groups A–F), not all 21. The extra 3 (group G) don't 
improve performance according to the paper itself.

So I made the following changes:

- Dropped group G from the default property list in AptaNetPSeAAC
- Updated pairs_to_features to accept a custom PSeAAC instance, 
  so users aren't stuck with the defaults
- Updated AptaNetPipeline to pass the custom instance through
- Added tests to verify the new default behavior and 
  custom PSeAAC usage

Old behavior still works — nothing breaks for existing users, 
the outputs just now actually match what the paper recommends.

Happy to get feedback or make changes if something looks off!

prs.title: Refactor: Removing global state reliance for thread-safe simulation runs
prs.body:
Hello gprMax team,

I've been working on an architectural refactor for gprMax to address a key piece of "architectural debt": the engine's heavy reliance on global configuration state. As you're working towards a major release on the devel branch, I thought this would be a great time to implement this improvement.

What’s the problem?
Previously, many core parts of the simulation (like the grid, model, and source definitions) looked at global variables or "singletons" to find their settings (data types, solver type, etc.). This caused a major bottleneck:

No Parallelism: You couldn't safely run multiple simulations in separate threads within the same Python script because they would all try to fight over those same global settings.
Hard to Test: Testing small pieces of code was difficult because they were "wired" into the global state.
What have I changed?
I’ve implemented what’s known as "Dependency Injection." In simple terms, instead of a function reaching out to a global variable for settings, the settings are now "injected" or passed directly to the function or object that needs them.

Key highlights of the refactor across 29 files:

Model-bound Settings: Each simulation model now carries its own configuration. When you create a Model, it gets its own sim_config and model_config.
Sources & Utilities: All source objects (dipoles, voltage sources, etc.) and utility functions (like memory checks) now get their configuration directly from the grid they belong to.
Thread Safety: Because each simulation is now isolated with its own configuration, this refactor is a direct prerequisite for enabling high-performance multi-threaded runs in the future.
Why does this matter?
This change makes gprMax much more modular and robust. It opens the door for developers to run massive batches of simulations concurrently in a single process without worrying about global state corruption.

I’ve focused these changes on the devel branch and ensured that the core logic remains intact while significantly cleaning up the internal data flow.

I would love your feedback on these improvements!

prs.title: Security: Implement a sandbox for #python scripting blocks
prs.body:
Hi gprMax team,

I noticed that the current implementation of #python blocks in .in files allows for arbitrary code execution on a user's system. Since researchers frequently share simulation models, this represents a potential security risk where a malicious file could perform unintended actions.

Even though these blocks are deprecated in the devel branch, I wanted to provide a "root cause" fix to ensure the community is protected until the feature is fully removed.

What this PR does:

Safety Scan: It now scans the Python code before it runs using the ast module. It proactively blocks any import statements or dangerous calls like open(), exec(), or eval().
Restricted Environment: I've moved the execution into a strict sandbox. By default, the script has zero access to standard Python built-ins. It only has access to a safe "whitelist" of functions (like range, math, and numpy) that are actually needed for GPR modeling.
Private Data Protection: It blocks any attempts to access private Python attributes (like __class__) to prevent sandbox breakouts.
This keeps the project's powerful scripting capabilities intact for current users while making it significantly safer to run shared models from the internet or colleagues.

Note: I've focused this PR on the devel branch as requested, specifically updating the new hash_cmds_file.py logic.

prs.title: fix: LocalCommunicationConfig YAML deserialization for tcp, shmem, and unixdomain (#1582)
prs.body:
## Problem

When users wrote this in their dataflow YAML file:
```yaml
communication:
  _unstable_local: tcp
```

dora would crash or fail silently. Same for `shmem` and `unixdomain`.

## Root Cause

`LocalCommunicationConfig` had no serde attributes, so serde expected 
PascalCase (`Tcp`, `UnixDomain`) but users write lowercase in YAML. 
`RemoteCommunicationConfig` already had `rename_all = "lowercase"` 
correctly — `LocalCommunicationConfig` was just missing it.

## Fix

- Added `#[serde(rename_all = "lowercase")]` to `LocalCommunicationConfig`
- Added `#[serde(rename = "unixdomain")]` on the `UnixDomain` variant
- Added `JsonSchema` derive for consistency with the rest of the codebase
- Removed the `#[schemars(with = "String")]` workaround (no longer needed)
- Switched to `derive(Default)` to fix clippy warnings

## Tests Added

- `tcp` → `LocalCommunicationConfig::Tcp` ✅
- `shmem` → `LocalCommunicationConfig::Shmem` ✅  
- `unixdomain` → `LocalCommunicationConfig::UnixDomain` ✅
- Invalid variant returns error ✅
- Full roundtrip serialization/deserialization ✅

Fixes #1582

prs.title: Fix memory accumulation in multiscene runs
prs.body:
## Problem

When running multiple scenes using `gprMax.run(scenes=scenes, n=len(scenes))`, 
memory from each scene was not being released after it completed. Each scene 
adds ~2GB to RAM and never frees it, so after enough scenes the process 
crashes with an out-of-memory error.

## Root Cause

After debugging I found 6 separate issues all contributing to this:

- `FDTDGrid` had no teardown method, so large numpy arrays like `Ex`, `Ey`, 
  `Ez`, `Hx`, `Hy`, `Hz`, `solid`, `rigidE`, `rigidH`, `ID`, 
  `updatecoeffsE`, `updatecoeffsH` stayed in memory after each scene
- `Material.maxpoles` is a class-level variable that was never reset, so 
  the dispersive pole count from one scene leaked into the next
- `Snapshot` class variables (`nx_max`, `ny_max`, `nz_max`, `bpg`) had 
  the same problem
- `h5py.File()` in `fields_outputs.py` was not using a context manager, 
  leaving file handles open
- Same file handle leak existed in `geometry_outputs.py` for both the 
  HDF5 and materials text file
- `model_build_run.py` was calling `del G` without first releasing the 
  large arrays `G` was holding

## Changes

- `gprMax/grid.py` — added `FDTDGrid.cleanup()` that explicitly deletes 
  all large field and geometry arrays and clears object lists
- `gprMax/materials.py` — added `Material.reset_class_state()` to reset 
  `maxpoles` between runs
- `gprMax/snapshots.py` — added `Snapshot.reset_class_state()` to reset 
  class-level dimension variables
- `gprMax/fields_outputs.py` — wrapped `h5py.File()` in a `with` block
- `gprMax/geometry_outputs.py` — same context manager fix for both file handles
- `gprMax/model_build_run.py` — call `cleanup()` and both `reset_class_state()` 
  methods before `del G`
- `tests/test_memory_cleanup.py` — 11 unit tests covering all the above

## Testing
```bash
conda activate gprMax
python -m pytest tests/test_memory_cleanup.py -v
```

## Notes

`geometry_fixed` mode is fully preserved — cleanup only runs when geometry 
is not being reused between models.

Fixes #[paste the issue number here]

prs.title: feat(cli): make progress reporting explicit and error-safe
prs.body:
### Summary

This PR hardens the progress bar implementation introduced for long-running CLI operations by making success and failure handling explicit.

Previously, progress cleanup relied on `Drop`, which could silently mark operations as completed or leave ambiguity on early returns and error paths. This change ensures users only see a success indicator when an operation truly succeeds.

Fixes #1229.

---

### Problem

Progress indicators were implicitly finalized via `Drop`, which meant:
- Early returns or errors could appear as successful completion
- Failure paths were indistinguishable from normal cleanup
- Success was not explicitly asserted

This is unsafe for CLI feedback, especially during orchestration-heavy operations.

---

### Solution

- Progress handles now track internal state (`active`, `success`, `failed`)
- **Success is explicit**: only `finish_with_message(...)` marks success
- **Failure is the default**:
  - Unfinished handles are treated as failed on drop
  - No success indicator is printed unless explicitly set
- Drop never signals success
- Non-TTY environments remain no-op (CI and piped output stay clean)

---

### Design Notes

- Progress reporting remains at the orchestration layer (no business logic changes)
- No changes to command semantics or error propagation
- ANSI output is fully suppressed when stdout is not

prs.title: Add invariant tests for NodeExitStatus classification
prs.body:
## Summary
This PR adds invariant tests for `NodeExitStatus` classification.

A full audit of the node lifecycle and exit-status handling logic was
performed, and the current implementation was found to be correct and
well-designed. These tests encode the expected invariants to prevent
future regressions across platforms.

## Scope
- Adds unit-level invariant tests only
- No runtime, daemon, or lifecycle logic changes
- No behavior changes

## Invariants Covered
- Exit code `0` is classified as `Success`
- Non-zero exit codes are classified as `ExitCode`
- Unix signal termination is classified as `Signal`
- `io::Error` is propagated as `IoError`
- `Unknown` is unreachable for valid inputs

## Validation
- All existing tests pass locally
- New tests are deterministic and platform-safe

prs.title: feat: add keyboard shortcuts for common actions
prs.body:
This contribution adds essential keyboard shortcuts to the s-console library, improving user experience and bringing it closer to standard console behavior found in modern browser developer tools.

Added Features
Clear Console (Ctrl/Cmd + L)

Quickly clears the entire console output
Matches the behavior of browser DevTools
Implemented through the existing [clear()]method


Instantly clears the input field without executing any command
Useful when you want to start over typing a command
Similar to terminal emulator behavior
Blur Input (Escape Key)

Allows users to unfocus from the input field
Helpful when you want to interact with other parts of the page
Improves accessibility and keyboard navigation

issues.title: Daemon-to-node backpressure semantics: queue_size appears ignored at runtime
issues.body:
Hi folks,

I’ve been running dora-rs locally and digging into how the daemon handles message delivery to nodes. While doing that, I noticed something that felt a bit off—and I wanted to check in with you all to see if it’s intentional or if it might be worth revisiting.

#The gist

Right now, the daemon uses **unbounded channels** to send input events to nodes. That means the `queue_size` setting in a node’s input config doesn’t actually limit how many messages the daemon can buffer for that node.  

In practice, if a node is slow to process messages—or even temporarily offline—the daemon will just keep piling messages into memory without any backpressure. Over time, this can lead to unbounded memory growth in the daemon itself.

# What I saw in the code

The daemon holds onto senders like this:

```rust
subscribe_channels: HashMap<NodeId, UnboundedSender<Timestamped<NodeEvent>>>
```

So even though you configure something like:

```yaml
inputs:
  image:
    source: camera/image
    queue_size: 10
```

…that `queue_size: 10` only seems to affect buffering **inside the node**, not the channel between the daemon and the node. The daemon-to-node pipeline remains unbounded.

### Why this caught me off guard

As a user, when I set `queue_size: 10`, I’d intuitively expect one (or both) of the following:
- The system drops older messages once 10 are queued, **or**
- The daemon slows down or applies backpressure when the node can’t keep up

Instead, the daemon just keeps pushing messages into an ever-growing buffer. That’s especially surprising if you’re coming from systems like ROS 2 or DDS, where QoS settings like queue depth usually imply bounded behavior and clear resource guarantees.

# What I’m wondering

- Is this current behavior intentional?  
- If so, is there a design reason we prefer unbounded daemon-to-node channels?  
- If not, would it make sense to align `queue_size` with actual buffering limits at the daemon level—maybe by switching to bounded channels or implementing a dropping policy?

I’d love to hear your thoughts! Happy to help explore fixes or alternatives if this seems like a gap worth addressing.

Thanks!

issues.title: node modules file
issues.body:
It’s generally a bad idea to keep node_modules inside your repository. Make sure to exclude it from version control.
Add node_modules/ to your .gitignore file (you can use the one that comes with Next.js as a base) before committing your code.
